title: Introduction to Functions
learningObjective: Determine if a relation, equation, or graph defines a function using the definition as well as the vertical line test.

faqs.question: What is a relation?
faqs.answer: A relation is simply a relationship between two sets of values. Any collection of ordered pairs (x, y) is a relation. Think of it as a rule—or even just a list—that pairs inputs with outputs. Every function is a relation, but not every relation is a function. Relations are the bigger family; functions are a special subset with stricter rules.

faqs.question: What is the difference between a relation and a function?
faqs.answer: A relation is any set of ordered pairs—there are no restrictions. A function is a relation with one extra rule: every input (x-value) must produce exactly one output (y-value). Think of it like a vending machine. If you press B3, you expect one specific item to drop. If pressing B3 sometimes gave you chips and sometimes gave you a candy bar, you wouldn't trust the machine. Functions are trustworthy—one input, one output, every time.

faqs.question: What is the domain? What is the range?
faqs.answer: The domain is the set of all possible input values (x-values) for a relation or function. The range is the set of all possible output values (y-values). A helpful way to remember: the domain is what you're allowed to put in, and the range is what you get out. When listing domain and range, we write each value only once—no repeats—and typically list them in order from least to greatest.

faqs.question: Why does a function need exactly one output for each input?
faqs.answer: Because the whole point of a function is predictability. In math, science, and everyday life, we need to trust that the same input always gives us the same result. If you plug x = 3 into a function, you need to get one specific answer—not two or three possibilities. That's what makes functions useful for modeling the real world: they give us certainty.

faqs.question: Can two different inputs have the same output and still be a function?
faqs.answer: Yes! This is one of the most common misconceptions. A function only requires that each input has one output. There's no rule against multiple inputs sharing the same output. For example, both students who study 2 hours and 5 hours might earn a B in the class. That's perfectly fine—different inputs, same output. The problem only arises when one input tries to map to multiple outputs.

faqs.question: What is mapping notation and why do we use it?
faqs.answer: Mapping notation (also called an arrow diagram) is a visual way to show how inputs connect to outputs. You list the x-values in one circle and the y-values in another, then draw arrows from each input to its output. It's useful because it makes the structure of a relation visible—you can immediately see whether an input maps to more than one output, which tells you whether the relation is a function.

faqs.question: Why is it okay for y-values to repeat but not x-values?
faqs.answer: The function rule is about inputs, not outputs. The rule says: each input gets exactly one output. It says nothing about outputs being unique. Think about a class roster—multiple students (inputs) can share the same birthday (output), and that's no problem. But one student can't have two different birthdays. That's the same logic: repeated y-values are fine, repeated x-values with different y-values are not.

faqs.question: How do I use the vertical line test?
faqs.answer: Imagine sliding a vertical line across the graph from left to right. At every position, ask: does this vertical line touch the graph in more than one place? If it ever hits two or more points, then one x-value has multiple y-values—and the relation is NOT a function. If every vertical line you can draw only touches the graph once, it IS a function. The key insight: vertical lines represent a single x-value, so if the line hits the graph twice, that x-value has two outputs.

faqs.question: How do I know if an equation is a function without graphing it?
faqs.answer: Try plugging in a value for x and see how many y-values you get. If every x-value you test gives exactly one y-value, it's likely a function. A red flag is when y appears with an even exponent (like y²) or inside an absolute value in a way that creates two solutions. For example, x = y² is not a function because if x = 4, then y could be 2 or −2. But y = x² IS a function because any x you plug in gives exactly one y.

faqs.question: Why does y² make something not a function?
faqs.answer: When you solve for a squared variable, you have to consider both the positive and negative square root. For example, if x = y², and you plug in x = 9, then y² = 9, which means y = 3 or y = −3. That's two outputs for one input—which violates the function rule. This is why equations like x = y² and x² + y² = 25 (a circle) are not functions. Anytime solving for y requires a ± step, be suspicious.

faqs.question: What makes a value 'undefined'?
faqs.answer: In this course, two situations make an expression undefined: dividing by zero, and taking the square root of a negative number. Division by zero is undefined because no number times zero gives you something other than zero. Square roots of negative numbers are undefined (in real numbers) because no real number times itself gives a negative result. When finding domain, you're looking for x-values that avoid both of these situations.

faqs.question: How do I find the domain of a function with a square root?
faqs.answer: Set the expression inside the square root greater than or equal to zero, then solve. For example, for f(x) = √(x − 2), set x − 2 ≥ 0, which gives x ≥ 2. In interval notation: [2, ∞). The bracket on 2 means 2 is included (since √0 = 0 is defined). Remember: the inside of a square root cannot be negative, but it can be zero.

faqs.question: How do I find the domain of a function with a fraction?
faqs.answer: Set the denominator equal to zero and solve—those are the values you must exclude. For example, for f(x) = 3/(x − 5), set x − 5 = 0, so x = 5 is excluded. The domain is all real numbers except 5, written as (−∞, 5) ∪ (5, ∞). If the function has both a fraction AND a square root in the denominator, you need to handle both restrictions: the denominator can't be zero AND the expression under the root can't be negative.

faqs.question: How do I write domain in interval notation?
faqs.answer: Interval notation uses parentheses ( ) for values that are NOT included and brackets [ ] for values that ARE included. Infinity always gets a parenthesis because you can never reach it. Use ∪ (union) to join separate intervals. Examples: all real numbers = (−∞, ∞). All numbers greater than or equal to 2 = [2, ∞). All numbers except 5 = (−∞, 5) ∪ (5, ∞). Think of parentheses as 'get close but don't touch' and brackets as 'this value is included.'

faqs.question: Why do functions matter—what's the point?
faqs.answer: Functions are the language of change. Nearly everything you'll study in this course—and in calculus—is built on functions. When scientists model population growth, when engineers design a bridge, when economists predict market trends, they're all using functions. The reason we spend time learning to identify and analyze functions now is that every major concept in precalculus (transformations, trigonometry, exponential growth, limits) depends on understanding how inputs relate to outputs.

faqs.question: How will I use functions later in this course?
faqs.answer: This section is the foundation for everything ahead. In the next sections, you'll learn function notation (a shorthand for talking about functions), how to evaluate and compose functions, and how to transform their graphs. Later, you'll study specific families of functions—linear, polynomial, rational, exponential, logarithmic, and trigonometric—each with their own behavior and applications. By the end of this course, you'll be able to analyze any function's behavior and use it to solve real problems. Every section builds on the ideas you're learning right now.

videos.title: Introduction to a Function (Part 1)
videos.description: What is a function? This video introduces the concept using real-world examples and ordered pairs.

videos.title: Introduction to a Function (Part 2)
videos.description: Continues the introduction to functions with more examples and the formal definition.

videos.title: Introduction to Relations and Functions
videos.description: Explains the difference between relations and functions with mapping notation examples.

videos.title: Determine if a Relation is a Function
videos.description: How to test whether a given relation qualifies as a function using ordered pairs and tables.

videos.title: Determine if an Equation Represents a Function
videos.description: How to determine if an equation defines a function by testing inputs for unique outputs.

videos.title: The Vertical Line Test
videos.description: Learn how to use the vertical line test to quickly determine if a graph represents a function.

videos.title: Use the Vertical Line Test (Ex 1)
videos.description: Worked example applying the vertical line test to determine if a graph is a function.

videos.title: Use the Vertical Line Test (Ex 2)
videos.description: Another worked example of the vertical line test with a different type of graph.

videos.title: Determine if a Table of Values Represents a Function
videos.description: How to check whether data in a table represents a function by examining x-values.

videos.title: Introduction to Domain and Range
videos.description: What are domain and range? This video covers the basics using ordered pairs, tables, and graphs.

videos.title: Find Domain and Range of Ordered Pairs
videos.description: Practice finding domain and range from sets of ordered pairs and determining if the relation is a function.

videos.title: Determine the Domain of a Square Root Function
videos.description: Step-by-step process for finding the domain when the function contains a square root.

videos.title: Determine the Domain of a Rational Function
videos.description: How to find values that make a rational function undefined and write the domain.

videos.title: Domain: Square Root and Denominator Combined
videos.description: Finding the domain when a function has BOTH a square root and a fraction — handling two restrictions at once.

videos.title: Interval Notation
videos.description: How to read and write interval notation, including when to use parentheses vs. brackets.

videos.title: Set-Builder Notation
videos.description: An alternative way to express domain and range using set-builder notation.

apps.title: Domain & Range Master
apps.description: Practice identifying domain and range from graphs, tables, and equations with instant feedback.

apps.title: Inequality Explorer
apps.description: Build fluency converting between inequality notation and interval notation.

guided.title: Explore the Function Definition
guided.prompt: I'm learning about functions in precalculus. Can you give me three real-world examples of relations that ARE functions and two that are NOT functions? For each one, explain which is the input and which is the output, and why it does or doesn't qualify as a function. Don't use the textbook examples I've already seen (like vending machines)—surprise me with something creative.

guided.title: Understand the Vertical Line Test
guided.prompt: I just learned about the vertical line test for functions. Can you explain WHY it works—not just how to use it, but the mathematical reasoning behind it? Then give me a tricky example where a graph might fool someone into thinking it passes or fails the test when it actually does the opposite.

guided.title: Master Domain Restrictions
guided.prompt: I'm learning how to find the domain of functions. Walk me through the thought process step by step for a function that has BOTH a square root and a fraction, like f(x) = √(x+1)/(x−3). Ask me guiding questions as we go—don't just give me the answer. I want to understand the reasoning.

guided.title: Debug Common Mistakes
guided.prompt: I'm studying functions in precalculus. Can you show me three common student mistakes when determining whether a relation is a function? For each mistake, show me the wrong thinking, explain why it's wrong, and then show the correct reasoning. Focus on mistakes with ordered pairs, graphs, and equations.

guided.title: Connect to the Bigger Picture
guided.prompt: I just finished learning about what makes something a function. I know the definition and the vertical line test. But I want to understand: why is this concept so important for the rest of precalculus and calculus? Can you show me one specific example of how the function concept shows up in a later topic I'll study, and explain why it wouldn't work without this foundation?

open.title: Create Your Own
open.prompt: Use the AI tool of your choice. Start by describing what you're confused about or curious about from Section FN1 (Introduction to Functions). Ask the AI to help you explore that idea. Share what you learned and what surprised you.
